name: Super Source (Virtual)
_source: Virtual node for network flow computation
_note: Connects to all source nodes with unconstrained capacity

name: Super Sink (Virtual)
_source: Virtual node for network flow computation
_note: All refinery_out nodes connect to this

name: Russia — Urals (Novorossiysk)
_source: PPAC/Reuters 2024-25: Urals grade (SOUR, ~31 API, ~1.5% S) exported from Black Sea (Novorossiysk) / Baltic (Primorsk). ~40% of Russia→India = ~750 kb/d. Routes westbound via Suez/Bab.
_note: Split from the former combined src_russia node so ESPO and Urals originate at their real, geographically distinct ports.

name: Russia — ESPO (Kozmino)
_source: PPAC/Reuters 2024-25: ESPO grade (SOUR, ~35 API, ~0.5% S) exported from Kozmino on the Pacific coast. ~60% of Russia→India = ~1.10 Mb/d. Routes via South China Sea / Malacca.
_note: Split from the former combined src_russia node. Pacific origin fixes the previously wrong-way (via-Suez) ESPO route.

name: Iran (Kharg Island)
_source: Hackathon baseline estimate.
_note: Added to resolve missing src_iran alias.

name: Iraq (Basrah Terminal)
_source: PPAC 2024-25: Iraq ~19% of India imports = ~950 kb/d. Grade: Basrah Heavy (SOUR, ~29 API, ~3.5% sulfur) and Basrah Light (SOUR, ~33 API, ~2.9% sulfur). All transit via Hormuz.
_note: Exports primarily from Basrah/Fao terminals; all routed via Strait of Hormuz to India.

name: Saudi Arabia (Ras Tanura)
_source: PPAC 2024-25: Saudi Arabia ~16% of India imports = ~800 kb/d. Grade: Arab Heavy (SOUR, ~27 API, ~2.8% sulfur) and Arab Light (SOUR, ~33 API, ~1.8% sulfur).
_note: Term supply agreements with ARAMCO for Indian state refiners IOC/HPCL/BPCL. All transit via Hormuz.

name: UAE (Ruwais / Das Island)
_source: PPAC 2024-25: UAE ~11% of India imports = ~550 kb/d. Grade: Murban (SWEET, ~40.5 API, ~0.6% sulfur) and Das Blend. Murban classified SWEET per API/sulfur threshold.
_note: UAE's dominant export grade to India is Murban from Abu Dhabi. All transit via Hormuz (Abu Dhabi/Ruwais terminals).

name: Kuwait (Mina Al Ahmadi)
_source: PPAC 2024-25: Kuwait ~5% of India imports = ~250 kb/d. Grade: Kuwait Export Crude (SOUR, ~31 API, ~2.5% sulfur). All transit via Hormuz.
_note: Kuwait Oil Company exports via Mina Abdulla and Shuaiba terminals; all via Hormuz.

name: Nigeria (Bonny Terminal)
_source: PPAC 2024-25: Nigeria ~4% of India imports = ~200 kb/d. Grade: Bonny Light (SWEET, ~35.4 API, ~0.14% sulfur). Key SWEET crude for complex refineries.
_note: Bonny Light terminals in Niger Delta. Reaches India via Red Sea/Suez or Cape of Good Hope reroute.

name: USA (Gulf Coast Offshore)
_source: PPAC 2024-25: USA ~3% of India imports = ~150 kb/d. Grade: WTI (SWEET, ~39.6 API, ~0.24% sulfur). Exports from Gulf Coast (Houston Ship Channel).
_note: Reaches India via Atlantic/Cape of Good Hope route (~30 days). Increasingly important SWEET source for complex refineries.

name: Oman (Mina Al Fahal)
_source: PPAC/DGCI&S: India sources ~40 countries; Oman is a long-standing term supplier (Oman Blend, medium/sour, ~33 API ~1.1% S). Capacity [ESTIMATED — residual-bucket order-of-magnitude from the ~17.4% non-top-5 share of 4.84 Mb/d; PPAC's exact country-wise table did not extract via automated fetch]. Refresh with exact PPAC figures.
_note: Mina Al Fahal is on the Gulf of Oman, EAST of the Strait of Hormuz narrows (~56.3-56.6E) — Omani crude does not transit Hormuz. Routed directly to west-coast refineries, deliberately bypassing chk_hormuz, unlike every other Gulf source in this model. This is the key de-biasing addition: a real Gulf-adjacent supplier immune to the chokepoint every other Gulf source shares.

name: Qatar (Ras Laffan)
_source: Qatar Marine crude, ~35 API, ~1.5% S — deep-Gulf term supplier via Ras Laffan. Capacity [ESTIMATED — residual-bucket order-of-magnitude, see Oman note].
_note: Ras Laffan is inside the Gulf; Qatari crude transits Hormuz like Saudi/Iraq/Kuwait.

name: Kazakhstan (CPC Terminal, Novorossiysk)
_source: CPC Blend (Tengiz/Kashagan), ~45 API, ~0.5-0.6% S — light, comparatively sweet grade despite sharing a Black Sea loading area with Russian Urals. Capacity [ESTIMATED — residual-bucket order-of-magnitude, opportunistic/spot volumes to India].
_note: Exported from the CPC Marine Terminal at Yuzhnaya Ozereyevka, ~9km from Novorossiysk — the same Black Sea loading area as src_russia_urals, but a distinct country and a materially lighter/sweeter grade. Demonstrates the grade constraint is independent of shared shipping geography, not just shared politics.

name: Venezuela (José Terminal)
_source: Merey/Boscan heavy sour blends. Reuters/OilPrice (2026) name Venezuela among the fastest-growing diversification suppliers as Indian refiners reduced Russian intake. Capacity [ESTIMATED — residual-bucket order-of-magnitude].
_note: Routed via Cape of Good Hope — same southern-Atlantic logic as the existing USA route; no Panama/Suez shortcut advantage for VLCCs bound for India.

name: Brazil (Porto do Açu)
_source: Lula/Tupi pre-salt crude, ~28-30 API, ~0.3-0.4% S (light, low-sulfur). Reuters/OilPrice (2026) name Brazil among the fastest-growing diversification suppliers. Capacity [ESTIMATED — residual-bucket order-of-magnitude].
_note: South Atlantic route via Cape of Good Hope is the natural path — no detour through the Northern Hemisphere chokepoints this model already covers.

name: Angola (Luanda / Cabinda)
_source: Girassol/Cabinda light-sweet crudes (~31-32 API, ~0.35-0.4% S). Named alongside Nigeria as a growing West-African diversification supplier. Capacity [ESTIMATED — residual-bucket order-of-magnitude].
_note: Routed via Cape of Good Hope only, matching Nigeria's real post-Houthi primary route (the pre-2024 Suez/Bab route is not modeled here to keep the addition tight).

name: Strait of Hormuz
_source: EIA World Oil Transit Chokepoints 2024: global flow 20.7 Mb/d. India-specific capacity set to sum of Iraq+Saudi+UAE+Kuwait exports to India = 2,550,000 bbl/day (all Middle Eastern sources transit here). Global figure used for context only.
_note: Narrowest point is 21 nautical miles wide. Only 2-mile-wide shipping lanes in each direction. ~40-45% of India's crude imports transit here per EIA/PS.

name: Bab-el-Mandeb / Red Sea
_source: EIA 2024: global Bab-el-Mandeb flow dropped to 4.1 Mb/d (from 9.3 Mb/d in 2023) due to Houthi attacks. India-specific capacity = Russia Urals route (750k) + Nigeria via Suez (200k) = 950,000 bbl/day. The Houthi disruption is already partially modeled in the risk baseline.
_note: Key risk corridor: Houthi attacks in 2024-2025 have already demonstrated real disruption. Risk score elevated in demo scenarios.

name: Suez Canal
_source: EIA 2024: Suez+SUMED combined 4.8 Mb/d globally. India capacity set equal to chk_bab (950k) as flows are serial on the Urals/West Africa route. Canal can accommodate up to Suezmax vessels.
_note: Connected to chk_bab in series for westbound-to-India routes (Russia Urals, Nigeria). The 2021 Ever Given blockage demonstrated real disruption risk.

name: Strait of Malacca
_source: EIA 2024: Malacca global flow 22.5 Mb/d. India-specific: Russia ESPO (1,100k) + ME crude to east coast via Indian Ocean (600k) = 1,700,000 bbl/day. Used by east-coast refineries (Paradip, Vizag) receiving ESPO and some Gulf crude.
_note: India's east coast refineries receive ESPO crude (Russia) primarily through this strait. Lowest risk chokepoint in our network — no active threat actors as of 2026.

name: Cape of Good Hope
_source: EIA 2024: Cape of Good Hope reroute flows rose to 9.3 Mb/d in 2024 (from 6.2 Mb/d in 2023) due to Houthi-induced Red Sea diversion. India-specific bypass capacity estimated at 1,000,000 bbl/day — constrained by vessel scheduling lead time and port throughput, not the route itself. Labeled [ESTIMATED].
_note: NOT a chokepoint — this is a BYPASS ROUTE. openness is always 1.0, risk_score always 0.0. Its higher cost comes from edge attributes (extra ~10-14 days, +$1.5-2/bbl freight). The EIA data shows this route is actively used today.

name: Jamnagar (Reliance) — Inlet
_source: Reliance Industries Annual Report 2024-25: 68.2 MMTPA combined capacity (33.0 MMTPA DTA + 35.2 MMTPA SEZ). Conversion: 1 MMTPA = 20,000 bbl/day. Grade: NCI 21.1, capable of 216+ crude grades including ultra-heavy SOUR. Classified SWEET+SOUR.
_note: World's largest single-location refinery complex. Located on Gulf of Kutch, Gujarat. Receives crude via Single-Point Mooring (SPM) at Sikka terminal.

name: Jamnagar (Reliance) — Outlet
_source: Same as ref_jamnagar_in. Initial inventory set to 3 days × consumption_rate = 3 × 1,364,000 = 4,092,000 bbl (operational buffer).
_note: Outlet node for flow conservation. Inventory drain rate = consumption_rate_bbl_day.

name: Paradip (IOCL) — Inlet
_source: PPAC Refinery Capacity 2024-25: Paradip 15.0 MMTPA = 300,000 bbl/day. Designed for high-sulfur (SOUR) crudes with advanced hydrocracker and coker units. Located at Paradip Port, Odisha.
_note: East coast refinery. Receives primarily ESPO (Russia) and Middle East SOUR crudes via Bay of Bengal. Does NOT have significant SWEET crude processing capacity — classified SOUR only.

name: Paradip (IOCL) — Outlet
_source: Same as ref_paradip_in. Initial inventory = 3 days × 300,000 = 900,000 bbl.
_note: Outlet node.

name: Visakh / Vizag (HPCL) — Inlet
_source: PPAC 2024-25: Vizag 15.0 MMTPA = 300,000 bbl/day. Post-modernization with LC-Max unit allows processing of heavy and bottom-of-barrel feedstocks, both SWEET and SOUR. Located at Visakhapatnam Port, Andhra Pradesh.
_note: East coast refinery. Co-located with ISPRL Visakhapatnam SPR facility. Grade: SWEET+SOUR after modernization.

name: Visakh / Vizag (HPCL) — Outlet
_source: Same as ref_vizag_in. Initial inventory = 3 × 300,000 = 900,000 bbl.
_note: Outlet node.

name: Mangalore (MRPL) — Inlet
_source: PPAC 2024-25: MRPL 15.0 MMTPA = 300,000 bbl/day. Processes crudes from 18-46 API gravity range (broadest range of any Indian refinery per MRPL disclosures). SWEET+SOUR flexibility confirmed.
_note: West coast refinery. Co-located with ISPRL Mangalore SPR. Receives crude via SPM buoy off Mangalore coast.

name: Mangalore (MRPL) — Outlet
_source: Same as ref_mangalore_in. Initial inventory = 3 × 300,000 = 900,000 bbl.
_note: Outlet node.

name: Kochi (BPCL) — Inlet
_source: PPAC 2024-25: BPCL Kochi 15.5 MMTPA = 310,000 bbl/day. Equipped with SPM for VLCCs, diverse crude basket. SWEET+SOUR confirmed per BPCL disclosures.
_note: West coast refinery, southernmost of the five. Receives crude via SPM in Arabian Sea. BPCL is expanding this refinery.

name: Kochi (BPCL) — Outlet
_source: Same as ref_kochi_in. Initial inventory = 3 × 310,000 = 930,000 bbl.
_note: Outlet node.

name: ISPRL Visakhapatnam
_source: ISPRL public disclosures: Visakhapatnam capacity 1.33 MMT = 9.78 Mb (conversion: 1 MMT crude ≈ 7.35 barrels). Rounded to 9.8 Mb; 73% fill assumed from MoPNG statements (~Q3 2025). Fill = 0.73 × 9,800,000 = 7,154,000 ≈ 7.17 Mb. Max discharge: [ESTIMATED] ~85,000 bbl/day based on facility design specs (no public figure available).
_note: Underground rock cavern storage. Located adjacent to HPCL Vizag refinery — emergency pipeline connects directly.

name: ISPRL Mangalore
_source: ISPRL: Mangalore capacity 1.50 MMT = 11.025 Mb. ~73% fill = 8.05 Mb ≈ 8.08 Mb. Max discharge [ESTIMATED]: ~95,000 bbl/day.
_note: Underground cavern at Padur outskirts, operationally linked to MRPL Mangalore refinery.

name: ISPRL Padur
_source: ISPRL: Padur capacity 2.50 MMT = 18.375 Mb. ~73% fill = 13.41 Mb ≈ 13.48 Mb. Max discharge [ESTIMATED]: ~160,000 bbl/day (largest facility).
_note: Largest SPR facility. Located near Udupi district, Karnataka. Serves both Mangalore and Kochi refineries via pipeline.
